data.title: Affiliated Managers Group Q1: Declines In All Key Metrics
data.description:
I rate Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. (NYSE:AMG) a Hold, for capital appreciation-focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. AMG provides investment management services to clients that manage money or asset managers. As the clients do well, AMG does well. Over the past 10 years, AMG has underperformed the financial services sector. However, in the more recent 5 years, the company has outperformed. AMG touts its strategy, which could account for the change in trajectory; however, the Q1 earnings results were poor, creating doubts that the company will continue to outperform. The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock area:

  • Last 5-year total return outperformed the financial services sector
  • $712B assets under management (AUM)
  • Focused on share buybacks vs. dividends to return capital to shareholders
  • Pays a tiny dividend of $0.01 per share which raises the question – why bother?

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Hold at 3.12 (as of 5/29/25), which supports my rating. Note the “D” rating for Valuation.

Investment Management Services

Headquartered in West Palm Beach, Florida, Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. operates as an investment management company providing investment management services to mutual funds, institutional clients, retailers, and high net worth individuals in the United States.

It provides advisory or sub-advisory services to mutual funds. These funds are distributed to retail, high net worth, and institutional clients directly and through intermediaries, including independent investment advisors, retirement plan sponsors, broker-dealers, major fund marketplaces, and bank trust departments.

Affiliated Managers Group was formed in 1993 with additional offices in Prides Crossing, Massachusetts; Stamford, Connecticut; London, United Kingdom; Dubai, United Arab Emirates; Sydney, Australia; Hong Kong; Tokyo, Japan, Zurich, Switzerland, and Delaware.

From the investor presentation, here are some of the clients they service in the alternative asset class:

Of the clients listed above, I'm most familiar with Yacktman Funds, Tweedy, Browne, and NorthBridge.

Q1 2025 Earnings Results – Terrible

On May 8, 2025, the company declared Q1 results, which were not good. The chart below tells the story.

While AUM grew from $699.4 to $712.2 billion, just about every other important metric was down appreciably. For example, Aggregate fees dropped –13.7% to $1.27 billion. Net income dropped -51% to $72.4 million. Earnings per share dropped –46.9% to $2.20.

These results were so bad that the company didn't include percentage change in the charts, perhaps to hide the fact. I can imagine the CEO had a tough time coming up with a quote to make it sound positive.

The few positive actions included a new partnership with Verition Fund Management and Qualitas Energy, together with Q1 investment in NorthBridge Partners, further diversify AMG’s business and broaden its participation in alternatives, in line with its growth strategy, and the company repurchased approximately $173 million in common stock in the first quarter.

Performance To Date

Normally, I look at a 10-year performance. In AMG’s case, it was so bad I thought I had made a mistake, so I redid the data. Here is what I saw. An investment of $10,000 in AMG over 10 years would have lost money, ending up with $7,986. This is in stark comparison to the financial sector (XLF) overall, which tripled to $30,460 despite going through various crisis moments such as Covid. Based on this chart, I was considering a “Strong Sell” recommendation. It is still hard to ignore once you've seen, it because it creates doubt about the company’s ability to succeed.

I ran the same chart but for 5 years. So, an investment of $10,000 in AMG held over 5 years grew to $25,290 which indeed outperformed the financial sector, as XLF ended up just short at $23,450. Sometimes companies have a hard time getting started, or they are going through a transition. In this case, the stock has definitely seen better performance in more recent years. Will it continue? Based on Q1 results, I'm not too sure. Either way, this company is not a consistent, reliable path to wealth.

Valuation

AMG pays a $0.01 quarterly dividend, with an annual yield of 0.02%. It has a 5-year growth rate showing a decline of -47.15%. Early in the company’s history, it paid a dividend payout as high as $1.28 per year, but starting in 2021, the company cut the dividend dramatically to one cent per share per quarter. It seems pointless to keep it at such a low level, in my view.

In the Investor Presentation, AMG showed how the Adjusted EBITDA grew at a CAGR of 5% from 2020 to 2024. Similarly, the economic earnings per share grew at a CAGR of 13%. While positive growth is good, these are not the expected growth rates from a company that is growing at a rapid pace. These are the kinds of charts to show when you can manipulate the statistics to make your story. 4 years is a short time, and these growth rates don't indicate a high-growth, high-performing company, in my view.

AMG’s peers are included in the chart below, ranked by market capitalization. Within the peer group, AMG stands out for having a "non-dividend" vs. an average of 4-5%. AMG does post the second-highest 5-year total return at 157% and a respectable return on equity of 13.7%.

Invesco Ltd. (IVZ) pays the second-highest dividend yield of 5.8% with a solid 5-year total return, and the worst ROE in the group at 5.1%.

Janus Henderson Group plc (JHG) pays a 4% dividend, has a 5-year total return of 118%, and a weak ROE.

AllianceBernstein Holding (AB) pays the highest dividend at 8.1%, with a strong 5-year total return and good ROE of 21%.

Victory Capital Holdings Inc. (VCTR) stands out for its super-high 5-year total return of 332.1% and 83% dividend growth rate. It's one of the asset managers to watch in this space, showing extraordinary growth. They manage USAA insurance.

Cohen & Steers, Inc. (CNS), one of my personal favorite asset managers due to their closed-end fund offerings, pays a 3.2% dividend and has the highest ROE of 31%.

In comparing the peers based on total return, below is the 5-year chart. Normally, I would use 10 years, but the chart gets strange as each firm has had various crises or opportunities which skew the data. Victory Capital outperforms, while AMG comes in second place.

Outlook

AMG is heavily reliant on client success in the investing environment. Realizing the U.S. is in a volatile period, it is still considered highly favorable to business and investment. Therefore, it is expected that investment-based businesses will tend to grow within the next few years with lower taxes on businesses and deregulation. For some industries, such as cryptocurrency, it is considered a boom time as the President favors the sector.

AMG identifies its strategy to drive growth and compound earnings with the following 4 pillars: Affiliate Performance, Organic Growth, Affiliate Investments, and Share Repurchases. In essence, they succeed when their clients grow. The company will also buy back shares at the right time, which is a good way to build share value, in my view. Clearly, they are less interested in the dividend.

Risks To Rating

The largest risk to AMG is macroenvironmental volatility that causes investors to pull back on investments, preferring to stay in cash. Lower market values decrease fee income on AUM. The recent tariff war has created volatility in trading, sending shares up and down like waves on an ocean. As time goes on, we will see the effect of tariffs on company earnings, which may put pressure on prices as earnings are impacted.

The second-largest risk is competition, in my view. AMG is competing in a highly competitive field, trying to protect its servicing niche.

Bottom Line

I rate Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. a Hold for capital appreciation-focused investors looking for growing companies in the financial services sector. AMG just posted miserable Q1 results, which doesn't create a great deal of faith in management or their strategy, in my view.

The company provides investment management services to clients and invests in certain sectors such as alternatives and long equities. In the last 5 years, the performance has exceeded that of the financial services sector, however looking at a longer period such as 10 years, they are dramatically worse.

The company pays a tiny dividend of $0.01 per share every quarter but spends a great deal of its capital on buying back shares. It currently manages $712 billion AUM. Going forward, it's unclear as to the direction of the stock; therefore, I recommend holding at this time.

data.title: Weitz Multi Cap Equity Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Against a challenging market backdrop, we were pleased to report “black ink” for the first quarter of 2025. The Multi Cap Equity Fund’s Institutional Class returned +0.81% for the quarter, comparing favorably to the Russell 3000 Index’s loss of -4.72%. The “AI Trade” was dealt a setback in late January after Chinese firm DeepSeek announced its advanced AI model had been developed at a fraction of the cost of U.S. competitors. This led to greater investor scrutiny of U.S. firms’ spending plans.

In the quarter’s closing weeks, investors began bracing for the new administration’s trade and tariff plans, set to be announced in the early days of April. Uncertainties such as these (particularly of this magnitude) challenge investors’ and business leaders’ confidence, resulting in a pullback from equities heading into quarter end.

Notable 1Q Contributors

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), the Fund’s largest holding, often benefits in volatile markets. Berkshire’s operations include a wide swath of American and global businesses that are surely impacted by changes in trade policy. However, Berkshire’s “fortress” balance sheet (including over $300 billion of cash) and strong record of capital allocation suggest the company is poised to benefit from broader market turbulence.

Aerospace parts and services provider HEICO (HEI.A) delivered standout earnings, featuring stronger-than-expected growth in both of its segments. HEICO’s value proposition of saving customers money by adopting their cost-effective, alternative parts remains as relevant as ever, and we believe it will drive growth into the future. Visa (V) similarly delivered solid growth despite cross-border volumes continuing to lag historical trends. Our Charter-related holdings (including Liberty Broadband (LBRDA)) results are better described as “good enough” relative to low expectations. Capital investments for charter should peak this year with improving free cash flow characteristics on the horizon. Lastly, investors rewarded LKQ (LKQ) management’s focus on improving margins, reducing debt, and returning cash to shareholders

Notable 1Q Detractors

On the negative side of the ledger, Alphabet (GOOG) was the Fund’s top detractor. The company’s Google unit is on the front lines of the “Traditional Search vs. GenAI” battle. With its own GenAI capabilities embedded in Google Search results, it’s helpful that Alphabet has a foot in each camp. Nevertheless, investors fear a significant shift of search share in favor of competitors, specifically ChatGPT. We believe the debate is far from settled and has overshadowed other strong businesses inside Alphabet (YouTube, to name one). We elected to reduce our position size early in the quarter to reflect the heightened competitive pressures.

Perimeter Solutions (PRM) reported a strong fourth quarter, capping an above-average fire season, yet shares were down in 1Q. Investors are likely anticipating a reduced wildfire experience in 2025 and are expressing some skepticism over the announced, modest-sized acquisition of IMS Corp, a maker of niche printed circuit boards. While far afield of Perimeter’s core business, management has long articulated a “platform” approach to acquisitions, pursuing different business lines that meet their criteria and provide additional verticals for organic growth and bolt-on acquisitions.

The demand environment for life sciences remains in flux as cuts to federal health agencies are underway and research grant funding is under question. In response, investors have shed shares of industry “pure plays” (Bio-Techne (TECH), Danaher (DHR)) and those with more modest life science exposure (industrial machinery company IDEX Corp. (IEX)) alike. Near-term uncertainty remains high, but we remain confident in the long-term opportunity and added to Danaher and Bio-Techne this quarter.

1Q Portfolio Changes

Portfolio activity was elevated in the first quarter. In addition to our life sciences additions, we initiated new positions in three companies. U.S. broadband giant Comcast (CMCSA) and leading consulting and IT services firm Accenture (ACN) returned to the portfolio after multi-year hiatuses following underwhelming earnings reports that created compelling entry points. We also purchased shares in IDEXX Laboratories (IDXX), the leading provider of diagnostic lab equipment for veterinary practices. Growth in testing volumes has slowed as the wave of pet adoptions during COVID moves into a lower utilization phase of life.

In the longer term, however, several structural tailwinds remain in place including growing pet ownership, increased adoption of diagnostic tools and underpenetrated international markets. Funding these purchases were trims of Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet, and LICT Corp. (OTCPK:LICT). We also exited our position in Veralto (VLTO), a profitable spin-off of Danaher, and completed our sale of Liberty Global (LBTYA) shares.

data.title: The Low-Stress Method To Boost Your Retirement Income
data.description:
Investing for durable and/or retirement income means different things for different people. We can, however, encapsulate these various approaches into three categories:

  1. Income growth focused.
  2. High-yield focused.
  3. Combination of both.

The logic behind going with the first category is to develop a portfolio that would produce growing current income streams at a rate, which exceeds inflation by a huge margin. The hope is that over time the total income would compound to something very meaningful, potentially exceeding the amount that would be there had the strategy been tilted towards the high-yield segment. An additional benefit here is that usually income growth companies are in a growth-phase, which tends to introduce capital appreciation effects.

The negative side of this strategy is that one becomes dependent on future growth, which might not happen if the business or macro conditions change to the downside. In such a case, investors are left with low-income and limited growth. Also, to do this investors have to have a long enough time horizon for letting the compounding do its magic. For many, waiting decades for portfolio income extraction is not an option.

The second category is a high-yield focused one, where growth is set aside and income is brought to the spotlight. The idea here is to buy assets at really attractive yields that can move the needle from the get-go. This provides an immediate optionality to potentially fund some living expenses from the portfolio income. Furthermore, investors can avoid speculating about income growth trajectories, and instead focus on making sure that the dividends are well-covered by strong and durable cash flows.

The drawback of high-yield strategies lies in the fundamental truth - i.e., the higher the yield, the higher risk. It is almost per definition that by investing in, say, 8%+ yielding securities we are speaking about elevated risk, which might not necessarily go together with prudent retirement income portfolios. Plus, if inflation suddenly becomes hot, then the chances are that the real yield of such securities fall as there is usually no income growth engine embedded in such businesses or securities.

The third category is what I think many investors have also implemented in their portfolios. Namely, it is a combination of high-yield and income growth. It strikes a balance between tangible current income and some dividend growth that would at least offset the headwinds from inflation. On top of that, by going in both directions, the investable security space broadens, which, in turn, allows for better diversification.

Granted, by combining both worlds, there is an automatic sacrifice in place for those, who want to have big dividends from the start and for those, who do not care so much about present income but are more focused on an income compounding story.

In my humble opinion, it is exactly the third category, which offers the best conditions for structuring a low-stress and high-income portfolio.

Low-stress and high-income method

Speaking of low-stress and high-income method, let's first define what we mean by that.

I hope that most of you will agree with me that low-stress investing in the context of a retirement income portfolio has to, in one way or another, reflect the following characteristics:

  • Extremely limited income cut risk.
  • Extremely limited income-erosion risk due to high inflation.
  • Sufficient amount of recurring income to cover the cost base and leave some cushion for emergency (unplanned) items.

The definition of high income might sound like a straightforward thing, but actually, it is not. In theory, the higher the yield, the better. Yet, as elaborated above, we have to balance it with the aforementioned "low-stress" aspects. In fact, we have to start first with meeting the "low-stress" aspects and then only transition to the yield thing. The question gets further complicated by the notion of inflation-protected income streams. So, it is not only about durable yields, but durable yields that move in tandem with inflation (and this is why it is important to choose "the third category").

So, with this context in mind, let me provide practical asset class and security examples that tick all of the necessary boxes here.

REITs: The beauty of equity REITs is that they offer three things: 1) durable income, 2) inflation-linked income, and 3) the opportunity to capture above-average yields. The durability and inflation-linked factors stem from the underlying process of how they create value, which is having long-term leases on their owned properties with good quality and diversified pool of tenants. These leases generate higher cash flows (yields) than the cost of financing, which allows them to capture decent spreads (profits) from typically non-cyclical assets. Also, in almost all cases, where REITs have long-term leases, they come with inflation indexation.

The question is where to play in the REIT space.

Given the investment context that was outlined above, the best place to capture low-stress and high-income REITs is the net lease area.

Net lease REITs operate with very long-term leases that not only offer CPI bumps but also pass almost all of the operational costs to the client, which nicely enhances the cash flow stability aspect. It actually makes them rather similar to bonds, which is why the yields tend to be higher here. The only material negative is that the income growth prospects are rather limited. But this we will solve through a different asset class.

So, my two favorite net lease REITs are W. P. Carey (NYSE:WPC) and Realty Income Corporation (O). They both offer dividend yields close to 6%, carry robust balance sheets (investment grade), and have sufficient margin of safety on the dividend coverage end (i.e., FFO payout ratios at around 75%).

Infrastructure: This is my favorite place to seek income as it has the income durability aspect and a more pronounced income growth potential than for REITs. The infrastructure business model is predicated on developing non-cyclical and CapEx intensive assets that are then contracted to top-tier corporations or public sector authorities. Think of road projects, power distribution networks, telco towers and midstream infrastructure. The beauty here is that the relationship between infrastructure owners and offtakers takes place through long-term contracts that in some way or another provide:

  • Long-term and inflation-indexed cash flows.
  • Price and volumetric risk mitigants.
  • Project internal rate of returns that justify the CapEx spend.

A large part of the infrastructure space is exposed to secular tailwinds that stem from, say, the AI (need for energy, data centers) and limited fiscal debt capacities (public sector being dependent to private finance).

So, in this context, I really like the midstream play, which is a huge enabler for stable baseload energy (on top of being just a system-critical asset). The typical yield here is between 6% to 9% for MLPs that carry investment grade balance sheets and have above inflation level distribution growth profiles.

My Top 2 MLP picks for income maximization in a stress-free fashion are Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and Plains All American Pipeline (PAA) that have one of the highest distribution coverage ratios in the game coupled with conservative balance sheets. The income cut risk for EPD and PAA is very low.

Income growth: Here my answer is very simple - the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). The idea of allocating capital into income growth stocks is to make sure that the overall portfolio income streams move up in line and preferably exceed the realized inflation rate. While REITs and infrastructure have an inflation protection embedded in their books through long-term contracts, in practice, the CPI links come with a predefined ceiling. So, it does not really provide a full protection. Also, we have to be realistic and assign a small probability that during the process there will be a security that registers an income reduction. A strong income growth asset could compensate for that as well.

Now, the reason for using SCHD and nothing else here is also simple. SCHD is a well-diversified ETF, which holds stakes in ~100 U.S. blue chip businesses that exhibit above-average growth trajectories. The single security risk is limited. Apart from that, SCHD offers access to decent income growth without penalizing investors on the yield front. For example, the current yield of ~4% does not seem like a notable sacrifice, given that since 2011 the dividend has grown by more than 10% per year.

The bottom line

For prudent income investors, there is a classical dilemma between maximizing yield and keeping the risk level acceptable. On the one hand, no one wants to invest more than what is necessary to hit the target. For example, the higher the income per each dollar deployed, the less capital is needed to achieve the pre-defined recurring income target. On the other hand, no one wants to deal with income cuts and the loss of purchasing power of his/her portfolio.

This is why, in my view, investors have to take the golden mean by combining defensive high-yield securities with some presence of income growth assets that could help tackle the inflation aspect.

As elaborated in the article, REITs, infrastructure and SCHD are three areas (although SCHD is not an asset class), where I see opportunities for investors to implement a low-stress and high-income strategy.

data.title: HP Inc. Q2 Earnings: Why I Am Not Buying After The Dip
data.description:
Introduction

HP Inc. (HPQ), which I last covered in March 2023, made its way back on to my watch list after the stock fell precipitously in reaction to President Trump's Liberation Day announcements. Although HPQ stock started trending downward almost immediately after Trump's election victory late last year, I wouldn't go so far as to conclude that the market was already pricing in tariff-related headwinds at such an early stage. Rather, it was the lackluster fourth quarter fiscal 2024 and first quarter fiscal 2025 results that disappointed investors and led to double-digit declines following the announcements.

Nevertheless, a well-known company whose share price had almost halved from its 52-week high by early April 2025 piques my interest. However, the relatively quick recovery to a level of around $30 before the tariff announcement did not trigger fears of missing out, but rather made me cautious. Knowing that PC makers are likely to be among the hardest hit by the tariffs, I saw no reason to rush into buying HPQ stock and instead thought it was best to wait for the first earnings release after the tariffs announcement and comments from management before deciding whether or not to seize potential upcoming opportunities.

Yesterday's drop to around $25 could represent such an opportunity, but in my view only at first glance. In this update, I would like to share my take on HP Inc.'s Q2 FY2025 results released on Wednesday and explain why I am not currently considering buying HPQ shares, but I am still leaning towards a bullish stance.

HP Inc. Q2 FY 2025 Earnings Review – The Good And The Bad News

HP's earnings for the second quarter of fiscal 2025 came in at $0.71 per share, $0.09 below the latest consensus and 13% below the prior-year figure. GAAP diluted earnings per share were $0.42, down 31% year-over-year. At the company level, GAAP earnings were down 33% year-over-year, indicating a small positive impact from share repurchases.

While the rather significant drop in earnings certainly warrants a closer look, it was encouraging to see that GAAP net revenues increased by 3.3% compared to the second quarter of FY2024. As can be seen in Figure 1, HPQ has clearly benefited from the pandemic/lockdown-related spike in demand for computer equipment, and I would argue that the company has managed the subsequent normalization of demand well. That said, stagnating revenues in times of high inflation are, of course, not exactly good news. At the same time, I think this is to be expected from a company that lacks a tangible economic moat and, along with its main competitors Lenovo Group Limited (OTCPK:LNVGY, OTCPK:LNVGF) and Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL), largely manufactures and sells commoditized products, as discussed in more detail in my first article. Finally, HP's significant exposure to printing-related revenues and, of course, a generally weakening consumer health should also be emphasized in connection with the company's stagnating sales.

Given HP's issues to grow revenues in recent quarters (and even accounting for normalizing demand), the recent tariff announcements are a second headwind that needs to be factored into the equation before considering the stock as a potential long-term investment. In such an environment, astute management is needed that keeps costs under control and maintains a healthy balance sheet to avoid rising debt service costs, which would be a further headwind to earnings.

Figure 2, which shows HP's quarterly gross margin, shows a first impact of tariffs. While the 290 basis point year-over-year decline looks pretty significant and was a major contributor to the earnings decline, the comparison to Q2 FY2024 is certainly not straightforward, and it's also worth noting that HP's gross margin only declined 31 basis points quarter-over-quarter. Nevertheless, a business with a gross profitability in the low 20% range does not have much room to maneuver, which is why I am rather cautious about the company and its stock.

Management noted on the earnings call that the tariffs represented a net headwind of $0.12 per share in the second quarter of fiscal 2025, with mitigating actions already factored in, such as cost reductions, but also an accelerated transition of manufacturing locations. I was pleased to read that management expects nearly all HP products sold in North America to be manufactured outside of China by the end of June, which will at least somewhat mitigate a potentially further escalating trade war between the U.S. and China. Sourcing components is, of course, a whole other story.

In my opinion, it is only reasonable to expect that it will take some time for the mitigation effects to take full effect. Therefore, I was not surprised that management lowered the full-year guidance quite significantly, to $2.47 per share (midpoint), which assumes that the current U.S. tariffs remain in place for the rest of the year. However, the almost 18% drop in guidance shows quite well how cost sensitive the company is.

Understandably, management expects significant cost savings in the coming months, and I think it's worth emphasizing positively that the now expected cumulative gross savings of at least $2 billion by year-end will not be accompanied by higher restructuring costs. As detailed in previous earnings releases, HP is currently undergoing a restructuring program that will cost approximately $1.2 billion.

Considering that computers are likely to be among the hardest hit by the tariffs, I was pleased to see HP report solid net revenue growth of 7.1% year-over-year for its personal systems segment (primarily desktops, notebooks, and workstations), which alleviates some of the pressure on the cost side of the equation. Still, the printing segment continues to show signs of weakness, with net revenue down 4.3% year-over-year and down 2.1% quarter-over-quarter. I wouldn't go so far as to call the segment obsolete, but it is most likely in a long-term decline. Therefore, and also given the fierce competition in this commoditized industry, it is becoming increasingly difficult to maintain good profitability in the segment, even though consumables accounted for two-thirds of segment revenues in the second quarter.

In addition to reducing procurement and manufacturing costs - which is certainly not easy - HP should be able to unlock efficiencies in SG&A spending. Unlike R&D, which has remained largely constant over the years both in dollar terms and as a percentage of net revenues, SG&A spending has increased significantly and accounted for more than 11% of net revenues in the second quarter of fiscal 2025, which is more than half of current gross profitability. Here too, the year-over-year decline of 60 basis points - which indicates the first signs of improved cost discipline - should not be over-interpreted due to the difficult comparison (Figure 3).

In view of the rather disappointing development in SG&A spending to date, I remain rather cautious and plan to review the company's cost structure again in three months' time.

Finally, in terms of financial stability, I'm not sure the decision to take on significant debt to fund large-scale share buybacks was the right one (at least from a quantitative perspective). Granted, the company continues to generate healthy free cash flow - $2.6 billion to $3.0 billion is expected for fiscal 2025 - but I generally don't like to see cyclical companies lacking a tangible economic moat take on significant debt. Since the beginning of fiscal 2020, HP Inc. has spent more than $16 billion on share repurchases, while simultaneously growing net debt from a very healthy $0.4 billion at the end of the first quarter of fiscal 2020 to more than $8.0 billion at the end of the most recent quarter.

However, given the well-staggered maturity profile (Figure 4), and of course, barring a severe recession and further deterioration in trade relations, I would argue that HP's debt remains manageable. The company currently pays out about $1 billion in dividends, so there is enough free cash flow to pay down debt as it comes due. In this way, HP can avoid an increase in its debt servicing costs, which would certainly be caused by refinancing the comparatively cheap debt maturing between 2025 and 2027. HPQ's interest coverage ratio is currently about six times its expected free cash flow for fiscal 2025, which, I think, is acceptable for a cyclical business.

With this in mind, I would prefer to see management focus on shoring up the (slightly) tainted balance sheet rather than returning to more substantial share buybacks - despite an increasingly compelling valuation (see Figure 5 and Table 1). Of course, the current blended P/E ratio of less than 8 should be viewed critically, especially in view of HP's significant exposure to largely commoditized and even partially declining industries, the lack of a tangible economic moat, and the consequently rather elusive earnings growth.

Conclusion: Why I Maintain A Cautious Stance And Am Not Currently A Buyer Of HPQ Stock

HP Inc. reported Q2 FY2025 earnings well below consensus, reflecting the significant impact of tariffs. However, the seemingly precipitous 260 basis point decline in gross profitability should be viewed in the context of a difficult comparison. On a sequential basis, a decline of 31 basis points was reported.

Management emphasized that the mitigation actions are starting to take effect, but investors will have to be patient until the full impact is seen. At the moment, management is focusing primarily on cost savings and adjusting the manufacturing footprint. It was reassuring to read that almost all HP products sold in North America will be manufactured outside of China by the end of June, which will reduce the impact of a potentially escalating trade war between the U.S. and China at least somewhat. Sourcing components is of course a very different story, so I expect HPQ stock to react quite negatively to any potential news of further deterioration in trade relations with China.

The company's balance sheet has seen better days, but I would not over-interpret the situation, especially given the well-staggered debt maturity profile and reasonable interest coverage. Barring a significant economic downturn and, of course, significant further deterioration in trade relations, I think HP can navigate the current environment without having to cut its dividend - also in light of the current payout ratio of well below 50% of free cash flow.

That said, I'm not buying HP stock after the recent price drop - mainly because I don't think we've seen everything yet in terms of trade rhetoric. As a manufacturer of computer equipment, HP Inc. is of course one of the companies most affected, and as such the stock will most likely remain rather volatile. I also remain rather cautious given the generally quite limited margin headroom and the still surprisingly high SG&A (despite the ongoing restructuring).

However, given the increasingly compelling valuation - price-to-earnings ratio of under 8, dividend yield of 4.6%, 12% free cash flow yield even after the guidance cut - I can see taking a small speculative position at a price closer to the April low. But make no mistake - HPQ stock is far from what I would call a deep value play, even at $25. The current blended price-to-earnings ratio of less than 8 should be viewed critically, especially in light of HP's significant exposure to largely commoditized and even partially declining industries, lack of a tangible economic moat, and consequently rather elusive earnings growth. HP Inc. is far from a great company, but given its largely stable free cash flow generation and reasonable room to run, it reminds me a bit of a tobacco company that doesn't have a meaningful smoke-free portfolio (see this article). Of course, HP Inc. does not have the pricing power of tobacco companies, but at the same time, it should not be forgotten that it is part of what can be considered an oligopoly in the price-conscious user segment (along with Lenovo and Dell).

With volatility back up a bit in reaction to earnings, I would approach HPQ stock via put options. However, as put options with a strike near the April lows ($21 or $22) and an expiration in a month or two are currently trading at a still unconvincing implied yield of only about 5% to 12% p.a., I prefer to wait for a further increase in volatility - possibly as a result of a broader market correction.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: abrdn Healthcare Investors Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The equity portion of the Fund fell (gross of fees) and underperformed its benchmark over the quarter.[1]

The top three detractors were Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT), Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) and Vaxcyte (PCVX). Sarepta underperformed after reporting a patient death from acute liver failure linked to Elevidys, its gene therapy for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy — a development that raised significant investor concern. Regeneron declined amid continued pressure from the anticipated launch of generic competitors to Eyelea, its leading ophthalmology therapy. Vaxcyte also weakened after the release of mixed clinical data on its pneumococcal vaccine for children, compounded by broader uncertainty around regulatory and government vaccination policies.

The top three contributors were Gilead Sciences (GILD), Amgen (AMGN) and Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX). Gilead gained on growing optimism for its twice-yearly HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis drug, which has demonstrated near-complete efficacy and strengthened investor confidence. Amgen rebounded strongly despite a weak end to 2024, with its shares rising even amid mixed sentiment around its GLP-1 antibody, maritide — an impressive move given broader equity market softness. Vertex advanced significantly, led by the Food and Drug Administration’s approval of Journayvx — the first non-opioid acute pain treatment approved in over two decades — alongside continued robust demand for its cystic fibrosis therapies.

NAV return data includes investment management fees, custodial charges and administrative fees (such as Director and legal fees) and assumes the reinvestment of all distributions. abrdn Inc. (the “Adviser”) became the Fund’s adviser on October 27, 2023.

For periods prior to that date, the returns reflect performance information from a prior, unaffiliated adviser. The Fund is subject to investment risk, including the possible loss of principal. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized.

Activity

During the first quarter of 2025, we made several changes to the portfolio. Generally speaking, the portfolio management team identified relative value differentials favoring smaller-cap equities over large-cap pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies. Additionally, concerns over pending tariffs led us to adopt a more cautious stance toward the large-cap pharmaceutical and biotechnology sector. Consequently, we initiated several positions in predominantly small- and mid-cap pre-commercial or early product launch companies. Examples of new initiations included Crinetics Pharmaceuticals (CRNX), Personalis (PSNL), Cidara Therapeutics (CDTX), Avidity Biosciences (RNA), ARS Pharmaceuticals (SPRY), NewAmsterdam Pharma (NAMS), Tempus AI (TEM), and Sionna Therapeutics (SION). We also made a new investment in a pre-public, venture-backed company, Abcuro.

We sold several positions for various reasons. Some sales were motivated by a lack of confidence in the companies’ forward growth prospects, while others resulted from failed clinical trials. Examples of sold positions include Biogen, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), IDEXX Laboratories (IDXX), Avantor (AVTR), 89bio (ETNB), Moderna (MRNA), and Marinus Pharmaceuticals.

Market review

US equities ended the quarter notably down. Against a backdrop of lingering inflationary pressures and a relatively buoyant labour market, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25–4.50% during the quarter. The central bank’s chair, Jerome Powell, reiterated the Fed’s commitment to sustainably lowering annual inflation – as measured by its preferred measure, the core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index – below the 2% target. However, the Fed aims to proceed cautiously, taking a data-dependent approach as it seeks greater clarity on underlying economic trends given persistently high inflation data and the potential inflationary impact of President Trump’s policies, particularly his tariff announcements. Despite this, following some softer US economic data, investors are now factoring in the possibility that rates could be cut at a slightly faster pace than previously expected.

The performance of the US healthcare sector notably outpaced that of the S&P 500 Index over the quarter. This was due to the relatively defensive nature of healthcare stocks, along with other sectors such as utilities and real estate that also outperformed. Investor risk aversion increased amid concerns that President Trump’s recent tariff announcements could prove inflationary and weigh on US economic growth. There were also heightened geopolitical tensions due to the ongoing wars in Ukraine and the Middle East. The energy sector outperformed too, supported by its perceived role as an inflation hedge, the Trump administration’s ‘energy dominance’ policy, and heightened geopolitical tensions. Materials also fared well, as fears of a potential trade war and geopolitical uncertainty supported gold prices. Elsewhere, the consumer discretionary sector was the main laggard, reflecting mounting concerns about the health of the US consumer. Information technology also notably underperformed, as the prospect of interest rates staying higher for longer weighed on the sector, given its large weighting to growth companies whose future discounted earnings would be negatively affected.

Outlook & strategy

With Donald Trump securing his second term, uncertainty around election results has been replaced with policy ambiguity. Tax cuts and deregulation are supportive of GDP growth, but incremental tariffs and immigration restrictions could mute these gains. This combination of looser fiscal policy coupled with potential supply-side shocks raises inflation expectations. As a result, we expect Fed rate cuts to slow in 2025 while we await increased visibility over the policy agenda and its resulting impact on inflation.

In general, the near- and long-term outlooks for healthcare companies remain favourable. Long-term demographic trends of an aging population should continue to support the growing demand for new healthcare products and therapies. Due to the healthcare sector’s generally defensive characteristics, its relative underperformance in the short term should position it favourably, especially if the US and global economies slow down.

Footnote

[1] The Fund’s blended benchmark comprises 80% NASDAQ Biotechnology Index and 20% S&P Composite 1500 Health Care Index. The NASDAQ Biotechnology Index is a modified capitalization-weighted index that includes securities of NASDAQ-listed companies classified according to the Industry Classification Benchmark as biotechnology or pharmaceuticals. The S&P Composite 1500 Health Care Index includes securities of those companies listed on the S&P Composite 1500 Index classified according to the Industry Classification Benchmark as healthcare. The S&P Composite 1500 Index is a broad market portfolio representing the large-, mid- and small-cap segments of the U.S. equity market. data.title: OR Royalties: A Solid Buy-The-Dip Candidate
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground.

OR Royalties Q1 Results

OR Royalties Inc. (NYSE:OR) (previously Osisko Royalties) released its Q1 25 results earlier this month, reporting a volume of ~19,000 gold-equivalent ounces earned in the period. This translated to a 15% decline from the year-ago period, impacted by lower contributions from Canadian Malartic (Canada), lower contributions from Mantos Blancos (Chile), lower contributions from Bald Mountain (Nevada), and no contributions from either Renard (Canada) or Eagle (Canada) that combined for ~2,400 GEOs in the year-ago period.

Fortunately, these sharp declines were partially offset by higher contributions from Island Gold (Canada), Lamaque (Canada), Eleonore (Canada), CSA (Australia) and new contributions from Tocantinzinho (Brazil) among other assets, helping to smooth the significant impact from Eagle heading offline (slope failure of its heap leach facility). And while quarterly GEO volume came in at just ~22.6% of OR Royalties' annual guidance midpoint, we should see a meaningful improvement in GEOs earned as the year progresses, benefiting from higher grades at Mantos Blancos and CSA, higher production at Canadian Malartic, and new contributions from Namdini (Ghana).

Hence, while Q1 '25 was not a record quarter for the company, even with a record gold price (XAUUSD:CUR) which made up most of its revenue, OR Royalties should report a record quarter in Q2 '25 with increased GEO sales and the record gold price.

As for OR Royalties' financial results, quarterly revenue came in at ~$55 million (+22% year-over-year), while operating cash flow surged to ~$46 million (+23% year-over-year). This was helped by much higher average realized prices of $2,863/oz, $32.71/oz and ~$9,800/tonne for gold, silver, and copper, respectively, offset by lower royalty portfolio contributions. As for GEOs, we saw ~68% of GEOs come from gold and 28% from silver with the remainder from copper (previously primary diamonds) with Renard offline and Osisko's copper stream at CSA kicking in last year.

Notably, these results were achieved with a sub $2,900/oz gold price, with the Q2 '25 gold prices averaging ~$3,160/oz thus far, with Osisko having the potential to generate ~$200 million in operating cash flow this year. These results highlight the resilience of OR Royalties' business model relative to its peers, with the temporary loss of ~9,000 GEOs (expected Eagle contribution at ~180,000 ounce projected production profile) not putting a material dent in the company's financial results. Further, this has been achieved despite significant delays in the planned ramp up of Mantos Blancos to 20k TPD, which would have helped to offset more of this production dip in its royalty portfolio if not for ramp-up delays.

As for OR Royalties' balance sheet, the company ended Q1 '25 with net debt of ~$11 million, with ~$63 million in cash and cash equivalents and ~$74 million in debt on its RCF. This net debt position excludes a significant equity in Osisko Development (ODV) and equity holdings in MAC Copper (MTAL), which it looks like OR Royalties will be able to monetize following the proposed acquisition of the company by Harmony (HMY) announced this week. Subsequent to quarter-end, OR Royalties noted that it paid another $30 million on its RCF ($19.6 million in net repayments in Q1 '25), with the company set to exit Q2 '25 in a net cash position after successfully chipping away its debt over the past few years.

Recent Developments

2025 Outlook

As for OR Royalties' 2025 outlook, 2025 is expected to be a softer year with OR Royalties' guidance midpoint being 84,000 GEOs (up 4% year-over-year, but down over 10% from its 2023 peak). As noted previously, this is largely related to the lack of contribution from Eagle, but also related to the outperformance of gold vs. other metals, which has negatively impacted its GEO sales. This is similar to what we have seen across other royalty/streaming companies and why Royal Gold (RGLD) has begun providing guidance for all metals vs. GEOs, but what should matter to investors is the trend in net asset value, cash flow generation, and asset margins, and that trend is clearly up.

So, while GEOs may be down, the company is set for another record year financially with the potential to generate upwards of $200 million in operating cash flow, increasing to ~$280 million in 2029 ($2,900/oz gold price assumption, ~130,000 GEOs). Further, while Canadian Malartic has seen a trend lower in contributions over the past several quarters, this should tick up in Q2 '25, with Agnico Eagle (AEM) expecting a better Q2 '25 from this asset with a step-up in production in 2027 with contributions from its largest East Gouldie deposit (Odyssey Underground) at the Canadian Malartic Complex.

Canadian Malartic

Digging into Canadian Malartic a little closer, Agnico Eagle continues to report exceptional results from this massive orebody and has continued to reiterate its view that this could be a 1.0 million ounce per annum asset. While this is excellent news for multiple royalty/streaming companies that have coverage on this asset, Osisko Royalties has the most significant coverage with its tentacles sunk into the asset in several areas, including a 5.0% NSR royalty and ~$0.30/tonne royalty on any ore outside of the original royalty boundaries and a 3.0% - 5.0% NSR on the underground deposits, with a 5.0% NSR on Agnico's largest deposit, East Gouldie.

This is arguably the most valuable gold royalty in the sector today, and it's surprising that a larger company has not made a run at OR Royalties yet to get control of this incredible royalty asset that will likely be churning outgrowing royalty checks well into the 2050s.

As for recent drilling highlights and development progress, Agnico continues to transform this world-class asset since it took over full ownership and OR Royalties is an extremely enviable position with a royalty of this scale on a top-priority asset owned by the sector's most aggressive driller. Starting with development progress, Agnico shared that Odyssey ramp development is ahead of schedule, with shaft sinking now at level 111 (1,113 meter depth) and on schedule, with the concrete foundation for the production hoist poured. As for the latter, Odyssey Underground is the gift that keeps on giving, with yet another new discovery made recently, the sub-parallel Eclipse Zone.

Starting with East Gouldie, drilling into the lower eastern extension of East Gouldie hit 27.4 meters at 5.3 G/T of gold (including 8.5 meters at 9.9 G/T of gold), as well as 17.7 meters at 6.6 G/T of gold (yellow circles). These hits are just outside of the current resource base and well outside reserves, suggesting the potential for further in the resource base at depth. Importantly, these hits are well within OR Royalties' coverage area to the west of the Rand Malartic Property line.

Meanwhile, Agnico also hit a thick intercept of 58.2 meters at 2.2 G/T of gold at 1,948 meter depths just below the ultimate depth of its current Odyssey Shaft under construction (blue circle), suggesting that economic mineralization continues at even further depths over very impressive widths. Elsewhere, Agnico intersected 59.8 meters at 3.7 G/T of gold (pink circle) at the newly discovered Eclipse Zone, a sub-parallel zone that lies 50 to 100 meters north of the east portion of East Gouldie. Finally, Agnico intersected 17.3 meters at 3.7 G/T of gold at 766 meter depths in close proximity to its shaft and just east of the underground development, with Agnico confident that it could add more reserves in this area by year-end.

Overall, this consistent exploration success continues to support the potential for a second shaft here that could push underground production upwards of 750,000 ounces per annum, all applying to OR Royalties' 3.0% - 5.0% NSR. Further, the 2023 LOMP outlined by Agnico Eagle is looking far too conservative for what is now a ~20.5 million ounce resource base (reserves + resources across open-pit + UG ounces combined)*, with it certainly making sense to pull these ounces forward given the rapid growth in the resource base here.

(*) Underground resources and reserves at Odyssey come in at ~18.5 million ounces (*)

In fact, the 2023 internal study was completed based on ~9.0 million ounces (2023-2042), but we've since seen proven and probable reserves jump to ~5.5 million ounces (~2700% growth), M&I resources dip to ~3.2 million ounces because of successful resource conversion and inferred ounces increase by nearly 140% to ~9.7 million ounces. To summarize, while is already a world-class royalty owned by arguably the sector's best operator, it's only getting better each year, and it's no surprise that Agnico has 20 drills going, with all resource/reserve growth from this drill program coming at no cost to OR Royalties, the beauty of its superior business model vs. operators in the sector.

Capital Allocation

Moving to capital allocation, OR Royalties announced a 20% increase in its dividend to US$0.055 per quarter or US$0.22 per annum. This has increased its dividend yield to just shy of 1.0% or over 2.2% for those investors that held on to their positions from the stock's major lows in 2022, with further room for growth in this dividend if gold prices remain above $3,000/oz. In fact, the current dividend comes in at just ~$41 million, representing less than 20% of expected 2025 cash flow generation, even in a weaker year for the company.

Hence, I would not be surprised to see a further increase in the dividend to US$0.26/share by year-end 2026 (especially if Cariboo heads into construction), which would still represent less than 25% of annual cash flow at current gold prices.

In terms of its buyback, OR Royalties has an NCIB that allows to purchase up to ~9.3 million shares or just under 5% of its outstanding shares, with daily purchases capped at ~73,000 shares (excluding block purchase exemptions). The company has not used its buyback program recently after purchasing just ~26,000 shares last year near US$16.00/share, and I think the company should be commended for this discipline and focusing on debt repayment. In fact, unlike many sector constituents that are now suddenly deciding to aggressively retire shares with their share prices up 100% to 300% off their lows and at far less attractive valuations, OR Royalties has always been very disciplined, aiming to use its buyback opportunistically vs. just buying back a target weighting irrespective of price/valuation.

In fact, unlike most of its peers, OR Royalties repurchased ~3.8 million shares (2% of its outstanding shares) near US$11.00/share at multi-year lows, an exceptional use of capital at the time.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"So again, we will be active if we see the opportunity and really be opportunistic about lowering our share count because, as you know, and we've talked about this before, this team in particular is very focused on per share metrics."

- Osisko Royalties Q4 '24 Conference Call (emphasis added).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"And while our corporate development team remains busier than ever and in some cases, on some really sizable transactions, we didn't announce anything major during the period."

- Osisko Royalties Q1-25 Conference Call (emphasis added).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Besides, OR Royalties shared it remains "busier than ever," and with there still being a solid pipeline of potential royalties/streams that can potentially be had at reasonable valuations, I think this is the far better use of cash than plowing it into share buybacks after a significant move higher in the stock. As for its ability to transact on these deals, OR Royalties had ~$510 million in liquidity when including its accordion as of the end of Q1-25, more than enough to move the needle if any deals come across the plate at the right price that fit its portfolio.

Cariboo Project

Looking at the Cariboo Project, Osisko Development released an optimized Feasibility Study, highlighting a project capable of producing ~1.8 million ounces of gold over 10 years at industry-leading AISC of ~$1,160/oz. This project has seen some changes from the prior study, now looking at the addition of a gravity circuit, consolidating all processing to a single facility vs. trucking to its existing QR Mill, and mining larger stopes. The production profile in the first five years is expected to come in at ~202,000 ounces, or ~10,000 GEOs to OR Royalties' account, and assuming the project is fully financed by year-end, OR Royalties' could see the first contribution from this asset where it holds a massive 5.0% NSR by early 2028.

That said, financing this won't be easy for Osisko Development with a massive ~$880 million capex bill, up from ~$137 million (+$450 million expansion capital) under its phased build approach previously. Further, sub $1,200/oz AISC when including a royalty of this scale in cash costs seems a little ambitious, but this doesn't matter to OR Royalties which holds a royalty asset and doesn't need to worry about where operating costs ultimately land, nor does it need to contribute any capital towards construction and maintenance.

Further, Osisko Development is currently sitting in a working capital deficit of ~$30 million and while this hardly precludes the company from finding a financing solution and the fact that Cariboo is fully permitted is a positive, it's early to be too optimistic in terms of modeling this asset just yet. The good news is that the gold price environment could not be more favorable and things have become a little frothier and certainly more favorable for developers in the sector in terms of prices paid in acquisitions and royalty/stream terms. Hence, if there ever were a time to get Cariboo funded under a cash-strapped Osisko Development, this is certainly the right environment for it to happen.

Dalgaranga (Spartan Resources) Acquisition

Moving to Australia, Ramelius Resources [RMS.AX] has announced that it would be acquiring Spartan Resources for ~$1.56 billion in the first hint of frothier acquisitions in the sector. This came in at a multi-year-high price paid of ~$672/oz on M&I resources, though the project included a mill, with a price paid per resource ounce of ~$495. While Ramelius certainly paid top dollar for these high-grade ounces and exploration ground west of its Mt. Magnet Hub (Checkers Mill), this is exciting news for Osisko Royalties that paid ~$50 million for royalties on Dalgaranga, Yalgoo, Glenburgh and Mt. Egerton, with the star of the acquisition being a 1.8% GRR on Dalgaranga where Spartan has delineated a very high-grade deposit in Never Never (~1.45 million ounces at 9.4 G/T of gold), an even higher-grade resource at Pepper, and a new discovery directly north at Freak.

Aside from a much larger operator taking over at Dalgaranga, this acquisition could also mean earlier royalty revenue coming to OR Royalties given that Dalgaranga sits just over 50 kilometers from Ramelius' Checkers Mill, with the potential to process material here ahead of the restart of its ~2.5 MTPa CIL plant that's on care and maintenance currently. To summarize, this is a material upgrade for this royalty with a much larger operator (future production of ~500,000 ounces) and the potential to pull forward cash flows vs. the prior outlook of late 2026 to early 2027 for first royalty payments.

Japan Gold Royalty Purchase

Moving further east to Japan, OR Royalties purchased a 1.5% NSR for ~$5 million on Japan Gold's (OTCQB:JGLDF) projects in Japan that are separate from its non-JV properties. This includes Mizobe, which sits directly south of the ultra-high-grade and massive Hishikari Mine owned by Sumitomo (OTCPK:SSUMF) and directly east of the Kushikino Mine that operated from 1914 to 1974. As for drilling on this prospect, Japan Gold hit ~144 meters at 0.7 G/T of gold, which, while nowhere near the grades of the high-grade epithermal deposits that it's hunting down, certainly suggests the presence of thick gold-silver mineralization in what's still very early-stage exploration here.

This new royalty that complements OR Royalties' portfolio of ~200 royalties/streams also includes the Ikutahara Project, where Japan Gold did hit very high-grade mineralization over a narrow intercept with a highlight hole of 0.45 meters at 1,395 G/T of gold. Ikutahara is in northeast Japan among a suite of other exploration properties and while also very early stage, earlier drill results are encouraging.

Overall, I think this is an interesting royalty purchase that certainly comes at the right price for an earlier-stage speculative investment. And while I would be shocked to see any production from these assets in the next decade, this is a low-risk but extremely high-reward investment to secure a 1.5% NSR given that it takes just over 100,000 ounces of total production to pay this royalty off (~1,500 to OR Royalties' account), but secures exposure to some of the most prospective royalty ground in Japan. And assuming Japan Gold manages to find even a mini Hishikari at some point with ~1.0 million ounces at 10+ G/T of gold, this will turn out to be an incredible investment.

Some investors might argue that this is a long shot and there is no guarantee that this ever pays out on the investment. However, the investment to secure these royalties make up roughly one week of cash flow for OR Royalties, and it is a more than reasonable way to deploy some capital in a high reward but more speculation fashion.

MAC Copper Acquisition

Finally, the most recent news is that OR Royalties will likely get to monetize its MAC Copper [MTAL] shares (*) this year, with Harmony (HMY) recently announcing the acquisition of MAC Copper (MTAL) for its high-grade CSA Mine in Australia. For those unfamiliar, OR Royalties holds a copper stream (2.25% - 4.88%) and silver stream (100%) on payable metal from the CSA Mine, with this asset also moving into stronger hands under a 1.0+ million ounce producer (similar to Spartan ---> Ramelius at Dalgaranga). And with over 20% of key shareholders confirming they will vote in favor of the proposal (absent a better proposal), this deal looks like it has a good shot at going through.

(*) OR Royalties made a $40 million investment in MAC Copper with the purchase of its streams, with the ability to use these proceeds to pay down the remainder of its debt later this year (*)

Aside from this transaction that puts CSA into stronger hands, investors should be excited by the potential for incremental production from the Merrin Mine, which is what's now referred to as resources above the 900-meter level, including QTS South Upper. While it's not yet clear how much this will positively impact ounces coming to OR Royalties under its copper stream at this copper-zinc rich area, MAC Copper management noted that it could be a "Woodlawn-sized" operation, suggesting the potential for 10,000+ tonnes of copper production and 30,000+ tonnes of zinc production, with OR Royalties benefiting from a current 3% stream on copper produced at CSA.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

"And so we've been asked, well, how big could this thing be? Not all of it's in reserve, very little of it's in reserve, in fact, not all of it is actually in JORC or SK 1300 resource. I think it's pretty topical right now, but this would be a Woodlawn-sized mine, but mostly copper with a bit of zinc as opposed to the other way. That's probably the most analogous thing I can give to people right now, just for the Merrin Mine, is that this would be around about a Woodlawn-sized operation. So you can tell why we're quite excited about it and why we're throwing more resources at it as quickly as we can."

- MAC Copper Q1 '25 Conference Call.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As it stands, it's tough to figure out just how much contribution will be coming to OR Royalties from these recent developments in Australia (Dalgaranga accelerated cash flows, incremental production from Merrin), but it's certainly an upgrade and suggests the potential for OR Royalties to outperform its 2029 outlook of 117,500 GEOs (40% growth) at the mid-point, especially considering that Cariboo (~10,000 GEOs) was not in this outlook but could sneak in if we see Osisko Development fully financed within the next 18 months.

Valuation

Based on ~190 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$24.90, Osisko trades at a market cap of ~$4.73 billion and an enterprise value of ~$4.74 billion. This places OR Royalties at the top of the pack in terms of market cap among its mid-tier peers despite it having the #2 production profile by scale, with it being over 30,000 GEOs behind its closest peer. That said, Osisko Royalties undoubtedly deserves a premium for its superior jurisdictional profile, with it having the highest percentage of net asset value tied to Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions next to Vox Royalty (VOXR), and the highest among its big-6 peers.

However, OR Royalties still trades at a very reasonable valuation on a P/NAV basis even if creeping up there on EV/FCF (~24x FY2025 EV/FCF estimates), sitting at just under 1.2x P/NAV vs. an estimated net asset value of ~$3.98 billion (5%). So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be fair (and arguably conservative) multiples of 22x FY2025 cash flow and 1.4x P/NAV (5%) for a growing royalty/streaming company with the top jurisdictional profile among its peers, the highest margins and an exceptional pipeline of development assets, I see an updated fair value for OR of US$26.90, pointing to a 5% upside from current levels. That said, I am looking for a minimum 27% discount to fair value to justify starting new positions in mid-tier royalty/streaming companies, even if they have a strong Tier-1 tilt like OR, with the stock needing to decline below US$20.00 to head into a low-risk buy zone.

Obviously, this doesn't mean that the stock can't head higher from here, but I prefer to pay the right price with a significant margin of safety or pass entirely, and while there are still a few opportunities out there in the sector at dirt-cheap valuations, I see OR as closer to fully valued today, at least based on its near-term potential. Longer term, and assuming the company sees GEO volume increase to ~130,000 in 2029 at a 24x free cash flow multiple and a $3,000/oz gold price, I would not be surprised to see OR trade closer to $40.00/share longer-term (year-end 2029).

Summary

OR Royalties had a slow start to the year in Q1 '25 on account of lower production at CSA, Mantos Blancos and Malartic, the significant impact of no ounces from Eagle, and royalty receipts being back-end weighted at Namdini. However, Q2 '25 is set to be another record quarter financially and OR has a clear path to 125,000+ GEOs later this decade, with significant upside on top of this figure depending on how development assets progress. As for nearer-term upside, OR Royalties is set to have another record year for cash flow per share, and NAV/share continues to tick higher with each quarter of drilling out of East Gouldie, a deposit that keeps on giving and could find itself with a second shaft sunk into it next decade, increasing hoisting capacity closer to ~30,000 TPD.

Given OR Royalties' position as having an industry-leading pipeline and arguably the best gold royalty in the sector, I continue to see OR as a top-10 way to get exposure to precious metals, assuming it's bought on sharp pullbacks. Further, I would not be surprised to see the stock ultimately taken over in the next couple of years with some of its peers working with stronger currency now, suggesting a second path to a re-rating for investors buying the stock on dips. In summary, OR's future has never been brighter and its multiples should only improve given the challenges present in less favorable jurisdictions. Given this setup, I would view any pullbacks closer to US$20.00 as a buying opportunities.
